text: Hi all! Long time lurker here. In my spare time, I've been working on a web app for generating a customizable UI solely from an OpenAPI spec of your REST api. Its in the _very_ early stages, but I'd very much appreciate any feedback nonetheless. App can be found <https://condescending-brown-89fb8e.netlify.com/|here>. Try out the tutorial and let me know what you think!

text:
Often, when people come across Glamorous Toolkit, the attention is focused on how we see the environment and how this can change programming. At the same time, it might be less obvious that to make GT work, we build a whole new graphical stack that has the property of always relying on one single rendering tree.

We now put together an article describing what one rendering tree means and the doors it opens for creating new kinds of 2D interfaces. I think this might be of interest for some people here. Feedback would be very much welcome.
<https://medium.com/feenk/one-rendering-tree-918eae49bcff?source=friends_link&sk=c0551af8504ee919c4d8c54c390eef49>

text: <@UE3KC0VK6> I’d try all of if it were straightforward. I don’t like Jupyter that much, so I am always watching out for alternatives. But it looks like HDF5 is a show-stopper. Some people report success compiling it with emscripten, but it’s a pain to compile and even more of a pain to use.

text: from the product perspective, I don't JavaScript is going to replicate the scientific programming ecosystem anytime soon, or for this generation if tools, ever. So the use case of Observable now would likely be something besides a full replacement of Jupyter, rather a way to make a Jupyter notebook more interactive or a way to present results better

text: I think this has important implications for anyone trying to create this kind of FoC user interface inside a web browser using conventional UI frameworks. The "conventional" UI is rendered using the DOM, while visualizations are rendered in a canvas, and now you have a problem.

text: I made it to enable quick demos of a REST API I was working on at my previous job, but it turned out to be more generally useful for non-technical folks to explore the API, do sales pitches, etc.

text: Come here to say thanks. Good production quality and I like the inline notes (such as the one for Inktober). Many questions are thoughtful and inquisitive. The podcast is in good stewardship — looking forward to the next episode on ORCΛ!
